source_title: Money Worries, Identity, and the Trap of Being 'Good’ (Coaching Episode)

paragraphs.speaker: bobby
paragraphs.name: Bobby Hobert
paragraphs.text: One thing that's been on my mind recently a lot is abundance and really trying to call in financial consistency with my business. I left my job at the top of January to go all into my coaching business. This has been in the works over the last seven, eight years. I drove Uber full-time for three and a half years while building the first podcast, then pivoted, working at Liquid IV full-time while continuing to build my podcast, my brand. It finally came to a point where it felt now or never. I left my career with some runway. This year it has been a rollercoaster of emotions. Credit cards are ran up, cash flow is really low. We're looking into maybe having to liquidate stock market stuff to just get by. I'd love to just explore, are there limiting beliefs in mind? I see the value that I bring. I see it in the interactions with people or clients. I just wanna see where we go or just anything that you can challenge me that may help edge me in the right direction.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: What makes you call it abundance instead of say, I wanna be filthy rich?

paragraphs.speaker: bobby
paragraphs.name: Bobby Hobert
paragraphs.text: A friend of mine called me out on this. I think it's me being scared to say that out loud, that I'm a bad person if I want to acquire a shit ton of money.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: How okay is it for you just to want? Say something that you want that you're totally, like there's no constriction in your body when you say it.

paragraphs.speaker: bobby
paragraphs.name: Bobby Hobert
paragraphs.text: I want to make $250,000 a year. Yeah, there is constriction in your body there. It's okay for me to want to help change the world. It's not okay for me to want this abundance of money or want wealth, extreme wealth.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: Who was the parent that you had to emotionally take care of when you were young? Typically what I see is that somebody who has this desire to help the world, save the world, a lot of coaches fall into this category, they had somebody that they were responsible for their happiness when they were young. It was a subtle responsible for happiness. Maybe I need to make sure they're happy so they don't get angry in the house, or maybe I need to take care of them emotionally because they're always depressed.

paragraphs.speaker: bobby
paragraphs.name: Bobby Hobert
paragraphs.text: I don't feel I had to necessarily take care of either of my parents. If I go a layer deeper, I wanna help people live and create a life that excites them just the same way when I graduated college. 99% went and got a corporate career, and I was like, I'm going my own path. I wanna help empower others to step in and create that life that they want for themselves.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: What makes you not deserve money?

paragraphs.speaker: bobby
paragraphs.name: Bobby Hobert
paragraphs.text: I haven't worked hard enough. I don't have the skill sets for it. The value I bring doesn't equal to the amount of money that I know I should be paid for.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: Can you point to any place in the world where somebody's financial gain is deserved or lack of financial gain is deserved by how much effort they put in? What makes it that you have to add value to be deserving? Look at me and say, Joe, you need to add value if you want my love.

paragraphs.speaker: bobby
paragraphs.name: Bobby Hobert
paragraphs.text: Joe, you need to add value if you want my love. That feels not true.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: Imagine you have a kid, the kid pops out, you look at the kid and you're like, not gonna love you until you add some value. Imagine that kid grows up with that dagger. How are they gonna be at making money or having abundance?

paragraphs.speaker: bobby
paragraphs.name: Bobby Hobert
paragraphs.text: That kid would be raised in a very needy person in the world. Always selfish. The times when the energy feels needy, it doesn't come. And the moments when I'm completely surrendered and just non-attached, it flows.

paragraphs.speaker: joe
paragraphs.name: Joe Hudson
paragraphs.text: The recognition just happened, which is, oh, right, I just need to stand in my value. And that became a to-do that you have to do so that you earn whatever it is that you wanna earn, which destroys the epiphany. What is it that you have to do to deserve love? Do you have to be who you are fully? How about who you are half? Do you deserve love then?

paragraphs.speaker: bobby
paragraphs.name: Bobby Hobert
paragraphs.text: I've always been super self-critical. My mom has always said that to me. You are your biggest critic. I just don't know why I put so much weight on my shoulders. Like a ton.
